summary:
The Nanda Dynasty (c. 345–322 BC) was the first empire in Indian history to unite most of the Ganges Plain under a single sovereignty, ruling from their capital Pataliputra (modern Patna) with an army reportedly numbering in the hundreds of thousands. Founded by Mahapadma Nanda, who may have been of low-caste or non-Kshatriya origin (making his rise remarkable in the caste-stratified society of the time), the Nandas accumulated legendary wealth and controlled the fertile Gangetic heartland.

The Nanda Empire's historical significance lies partly in what it provoked: the scale of its military power (Greek sources report 200,000 infantry, 20,000 cavalry, 2,000 chariots, and 3,000–6,000 war elephants) caused Alexander the Great's troops to mutiny at the Hyphasis River (326 BC) and refuse to march further east — they would not face such a force after years of exhausting campaigning. The last Nanda king, Dhana Nanda, was overthrown by Chandragupta Maurya, assisted by the strategist Kautilya (author of the [[event:1108|Arthashastra of Kautilya]]), establishing the Maurya Empire around 322 BC. The Nandas' efficient revenue extraction and administrative system was inherited and refined by the Mauryas.
